perturbation.original_text: 1.3 "Affiliate" means, with respect to a Party, any corporation or other entity that is directly or indirectly controlling, controlled by or under the common control with such Party. For the purpose of this definition, "control" means the direct or indirect ownership of at least fifty percent (50%) of the outstanding shares or other voting rights of the subject entity to elect directors (or, in the case of an entity that is not a corporation, for the election of the corresponding managing authority), or if not meeting the preceding, any entity owned or controlled by or owning or controlling at the maximum control or ownership right permitted in the country where such entity exists.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 "Affiliate" means, with respect to a Party, any entity that is affiliated with, works in conjunction with, or has a strategic partnership with such Party. For the purpose of this definition, "control" is not required, and affiliation can be established through mutual agreements or collaborative projects. The extent of rights and obligations of an affiliate will be determined on a case-by-case basis by the Party involved.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by changing the definition of "Affiliate" to include strategic partnerships and loosely affiliated entities, removing the requirement of 50% ownership. This contradicts the original definition, which specifies that 'control' means at least 50% ownership. This contradiction creates ambiguity about which entities qualify as affiliates and what rights and obligations they have under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Joint Venture Company shall not, and shall cause each SPV and each of their Affiliates not to, directly or indirectly, market or sell any Loop-branded Products or any Products produced using Licensed Subject Matter to any person or entity except to Authorized Customers pursuant to a Transferred Contract (the "Marketing and Sale Restriction"); provided that if at any time during the term of this Agreement, Loop has delivered Proposed Contract Notices that result in Transferred Contracts having binding purchase and supply obligations for less than [***] of the then existing actual production capacity of a Licensed Facility (taking into account Joint Venture Company's reasonable forecasts based on customer requirements, seasonal variations, and other factors) in the subsequent three months (the "[***]"), Joint Venture Company may market and solicit orders for Licensed Products produced in such Licensed Facility directly to customers and potential customers and enter into supply contracts for the sale of Loop-branded Products or any Products produced using Licensed Subject Matter produced in such Licensed Facility with customers without being restricted by the Marketing and Sale Restriction. If at any subsequent time during the term of this Agreement Loop gives Joint Venture Party written notice that Loop is then capable of meeting, and is prepared to meet, the [***] and provides reasonable supporting information and documentation to Joint Venture Party with such notice, then at a date selected by Joint Venture Company that is within ninety (90) days after Joint Venture Company receives such written notice, the Market and Sale Restriction and the other provisions of this Section 2.7 shall go back into force. Nothing in this Section 2.7 shall relieve Loop of any of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original definition of "Affiliate" required a minimum of 50% ownership, providing a clear and objective criterion. The changed text broadens the definition to include entities with strategic partnerships and other less defined affiliations, removing the 50% ownership requirement. This directly contradicts the section that restricts Joint Venture Company, SPVs, and their Affiliates from certain marketing and sales activities. The contradicted section relies on a clear understanding of who constitutes an 'Affiliate' to enforce the restrictions. The modified definition introduces ambiguity, making it unclear whether a strategic partner or entity with a loose affiliation falls under the marketing and sales restriction, thus hindering enforcement and creating a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 Acceptance of Proposed Contracts. All Eligible Contracts shall be deemed accepted by the Joint Venture Company and become a Transferred Contract on the date Loop delivers the Proposed Contract Notice for such Eligible Contract to the Joint Venture Company. In the event that the Proposed Contract Notice is a Considered Contract, the Joint Venture Company will have the right to review and determine whether to accept or reject the Considered Contract within ten (10) days following Joint Venture Company's receipt of the Proposed Contract Notice (the "Review Period"). Joint Venture Company shall provide Loop with a written acceptance or rejection of each Considered Contract ("Acceptance Notice" or "Rejection Notice," as applicable) within the Review Period, which acceptance or rejection shall be in Joint Venture Company's sole discretion. If Joint Venture Company does not provide an Acceptance Notice or Rejection Notice to Loop within the Review Period, the Considered Contract will be deemed rejected by Joint Venture Company. Loop shall be entitled to present any rejected Considered Contract (whether deemed rejected or by receipt of a Rejection Notice) to an Alternate Producer on the same terms and conditions as those presented to Joint Venture Company. If Joint Venture Company provides an Acceptance Notice to Loop with respect to a Considered Contract during the Review Period, that Considered Contract shall become a Transferred Contract on the date Joint Venture Company provides that Acceptance Notice.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 Acceptance of Proposed Contracts. All Eligible Contracts shall be deemed accepted by the Joint Venture Company and become a Transferred Contract on the date Loop delivers the Proposed Contract Notice for such Eligible Contract to the Joint Venture Company. In the event that the Proposed Contract Notice is a Considered Contract, the Joint Venture Company will have the right to review and determine whether to accept or reject the Considered Contract within twenty (20) days following Joint Venture Company's receipt of the Proposed Contract Notice (the "Review Period"). Joint Venture Company shall provide Loop with a written acceptance or rejection of each Considered Contract ("Acceptance Notice" or "Rejection Notice," as applicable) within the Review Period, which acceptance or rejection shall be in Joint Venture Company's sole discretion. If Joint Venture Company does not provide an Acceptance Notice or Rejection Notice to Loop within the Review Period, the Considered Contract will be deemed accepted by Joint Venture Company. Loop shall be entitled to present any rejected Considered Contract (whether deemed rejected or by receipt of a Rejection Notice) to an Alternate Producer on the same terms and conditions as those presented to Joint Venture Company. If Joint Venture Company provides an Acceptance Notice to Loop with respect to a Considered Contract during the Review Period, that Considered Contract shall become a Transferred Contract on the date Joint Venture Company provides that Acceptance Notice.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by stating the Joint Venture Company has twenty (20) days to review and determine whether to accept or reject the Considered Contract, while also states 'If Joint Venture Company does not provide an Acceptance Notice or Rejection Notice to Loop within the Review Period, the Considered Contract will be deemed accepted by Joint Venture Company'. This creates ambiguity regarding whether Joint Venture Company has the ability to reject Considered Contracts and increases the review period from ten (10) days to twenty (20) days. This contradiction introduces ambiguity about whether Considered Contracts can be rejected, what is the review period and what happens when there is no response after the alloted review period.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Acceptance of Proposed Contracts. All Eligible Contracts shall be deemed accepted by the Joint Venture Company and become a Transferred Contract on the date Loop delivers the Proposed Contract Notice for such Eligible Contract to the Joint Venture Company. In the event that the Proposed Contract Notice is a Considered Contract, the Joint Venture Company will have the right to review and determine whether to accept or reject the Considered Contract within twenty (20) days following Joint Venture Company's receipt of the Proposed Contract Notice (the "Review Period"). Joint Venture Company shall provide Loop with a written acceptance or rejection of each Considered Contract ("Acceptance Notice" or "Rejection Notice," as applicable) within the Review Period, which acceptance or rejection shall be in Joint Venture Company's sole discretion. If Joint Venture Company does not provide an Acceptance Notice or Rejection Notice to Loop within the Review Period, the Considered Contract will be deemed accepted by Joint Venture Company. Loop shall be entitled to present any rejected Considered Contract (whether deemed rejected or by receipt of a Rejection Notice) to an Alternate Producer on the same terms and conditions as those presented to Joint Venture Company. If Joint Venture Company provides an Acceptance Notice to Loop with respect to a Considered Contract during the Review Period, that Considered Contract shall become a Transferred Contract on the date Joint Venture Company provides that Acceptance Notice.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction. The original text defined the Review Period as ten (10) days. The changed text modifies the Review Period to twenty (20) days and states, 'If Joint Venture Company does not provide an Acceptance Notice or Rejection Notice to Loop within the Review Period, the Considered Contract will be deemed accepted by Joint Venture Company'. This directly contradicts the earlier statement that the Joint Venture Company has the right to reject the Considered Contract, because if the company doesn't respond, it is deemed accepted. Therefore, the changed text creates a strong in-text contradiction regarding the ability to reject Considered Contracts.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Termination for Breach. If a Party commits a material breach of this Agreement, the non-breaching Party shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement if the breaching party does not cure the breach within thirty (30) days after the non- breaching party gives the breaching party written notice of the specific breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Termination for Breach. If a Party commits a material breach of this Agreement, the non-breaching Party shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement immediately. However, the breaching party shall have ninety (90) days to dispute the breach.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by stating that the non-breaching party is entitled to terminate the agreement immediately if a material breach occurs, while simultaneously granting the breaching party ninety (90) days to dispute the breach. This creates ambiguity regarding the timing of termination and the breaching party's rights. This in-text contradiction could lead to disputes over whether the termination was valid and whether the breaching party had sufficient time to rectify the alleged breach. It contradicts the original condition for termination with an immediate effect with the right of cure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Termination for Breach. If a Party commits a material breach of this Agreement, the non-breaching Party shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement immediately. However, the breaching party shall have ninety (90) days to dispute the breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction. It states that the non-breaching party can terminate the agreement 'immediately' upon a material breach, while also granting the breaching party 'ninety (90) days to dispute the breach.' This creates a clear conflict because immediate termination is incompatible with a 90-day dispute period. The original text allows for termination after a 30-day cure period, which is consistent. The contradicted text is the changed text itself, which makes it an in-text contradiction, as defined by 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.
